data.title: CHI: Premium Moderates For This Convertible Fund
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Calamos Convertible Opportunities and Income Fund (NASDAQ:CHI) has been a fund I've owned in the past, but I sold out of the position in early 2023 when the fund's premium started to get into the double digits. We also last wrote on this fund in early 2024, and at that time, the premium did decline somewhat. However, the fund has continued to see its premium come down since then, and it looks like a more interesting consideration at this time. While waiting for a discount would provide an even better bargain, one considering convertible and high-yield exposure could consider an initial entry.

CHI Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -1.50.
  • Discount/Premium: 1.09%.
  • Distribution Yield: 11.19%.
  • Expense Ratio: 1.36%.
  • Leverage: 34.32%.
  • Managed Assets: $1.236 billion.
  • Structure: Perpetual.

CHI states its objective as "seeks total return through capital appreciation and current income by investing in a diversified portfolio of convertible securities and high yield corporate bonds." This is fairly straightforward, but it can also be beneficial to retail investors who wouldn't normally have as easy access to convertible securities. Some are available, but most are only accessible to qualified institutional buyers.

The fund is leveraged, and rather highly leveraged at that. Generally, it isn't too much of an issue thanks to the fixed-income exposure of the fund. Still, this increases risks, and also the fund's total expense ratio climbs to 4.20%.

The fund is leveraged through mandatory redeemable preferred shares and a liquidity agreement. The dividend rate on these preferreds is quite low, but they unfortunately come with a redemption date in the next year or two, at a time when the replacements are having to issue higher dividend rates. For example, Series G is the latest, issued in the fall of 2024, and offers a 6.24% yield.

For the liquidity agreement, or "notes payable," the rate is based on a floating rate of OBFR plus 0.52%. As of the latest semi-annual report, the cost comes in at an average of 4.99%. When the Fed cut 100 basis points, that helped to ease the costs on this form of borrowing—and further potential rate cuts would also help.

Performance - Premium Moderates

Since our last update, the fund's total returns have been relatively mediocre when compared to the S&P 500 Index.

With convertible exposure, comparing to an equity benchmark can be more appropriate due to the convertibles' hybrid equity and fixed-income approach. That said, the S&P 500 Index during this time was mostly driven by the mega-cap names in tech, mostly the Magnificent 7. Without significant exposure to those names, we should expect to see more modest total returns on a relative basis.

When comparing the fund to the iShares Convertible Bond ETF (ICVT), a non-leveraged ETF barometer for the convertible investment space, we see that CHI underperformed. That was on a total NAV return basis, which is the portfolio's underlying performance, but that was to a lesser degree. Instead, we see the most drastic difference on a total share price return basis.

That's where the closed-end fund discount/premium comes into the picture. As CHI's premium has deflated, starting mostly at the start of this year, we've seen a rather material divergence. However, that is also what makes it a more interesting choice at this time.

Looking back over the longer-term history, we still aren't seeing the fund at a bargain. Instead, it is trading relatively close to its longer-term average level. That level was helped to be pushed higher by the rather persistent premium over the last several years.

Additionally, the -1.45 1-year z-score suggests that the fund is relatively undervalued on a shorter-term basis.

Distribution - High Rate Paid Monthly

At this time, the fund's distribution rate comes to 11.19%. Given the fund is trading right around its NAV, the underlying NAV distribution yield comes in similarly at 11.30%. Calamos tends to keep its payouts at level distributions for lengthy periods of time. In other words, they don't tend to make too many adjustments, and that is often considered attractive for income investors.

Of course, that doesn't tell us whether they are earning their distribution or not. To determine that, one would have to watch NAV over time. If it is trending lower, that would mean they aren't covering the payout, and if it is flat or rising, it would suggest they are. This is because the fund will need to produce coverage primarily through capital gains in the underlying portfolio.

With a higher interest rate environment, zero-coupon convertible bonds can become less appealing. Companies will generally have to offer a bit of a coupon to make a more compelling investment argument. At the same time, companies can offer lower relative yields for convertibles due to the potential conversion feature that can make them more appealing. That can make it a more enticing option for issuing these types of securities for the company.

With that said, the fund's net investment income is still negative, meaning the entire distribution will need to be covered through gains in the underlying portfolio. That's even considering the fund carries a nearly 25% allocation to high-yield bonds.

One thing about a fund trading at a premium that is beneficial is the ability to issue shares through an at-the-market offering or the DRIP. When it is done at a premium, that is accretive to NAV, which is something CHI has been able to benefit from.

On a per-share basis, it didn't amount to too much, but at $0.02, it also isn't nothing. As the premium has come down to around parity with the NAV, this won't be a lever that will amount to anything meaningful for now.

For tax purposes, the 2024 distribution saw quite a mixture of ordinary income, return of capital, short-term capital gains, long-term capital gains, and qualified dividends. Whether this fund would be best in a taxable or tax-advantaged account will be on a case-by-case basis. However, in general, it isn't necessarily as tax-efficient as other funds available.

CHI's Portfolio

As noted, this fund is primarily exposed to convertible securities, but also has a rather material sleeve of high-yield fixed income as well. Going back to our last coverage showing the breakdown over a year and a half ago, the allocation was virtually identical. The convertible sleeve comes in at 67.44% of the fund, when it was previously 67.40%.

However, what has increased quite materially is the fund's information technology sector allocation. It had been the largest allocation of the fund, but it was at 18.9%, and consumer discretionary was the second-highest allocation at 17.3%. The divergence between the two sleeves of this allocation is quite material.

At the same time, we still don't see any Mag 7 exposure. Instead, I see primarily a lot of the newcomers to the technology space. This is to be expected because these are companies that can offer the most potential for growth or capital appreciation going forward.

That can make their convertible issuances more appealing relative to larger companies, which have mostly matured. Further, mostly below-investment-grade-rated companies issue convertibles because larger companies avoid the potential dilution and can often find other ways to raise the capital needed.

In looking at the quality allocation, we can see that the highest allocation is to unrated securities. This goes back to most of these issuances being sold to only qualified institutional buyers. Forgoing the rating process can save costs and money. It is then up to companies such as Calamos and their managers to determine their own internal ratings and whether an investment may be worthwhile.

Conclusion

CHI isn't necessarily a bargain, but the fund's premium has come down to a more modest level. That could suggest a time when investors could begin to consider potentially putting some capital to work. However, one may also consider waiting for an overall market pullback before considering investing. While CHI isn't heavily exposed to the Magnificent 7 names that have generated the bulk of the returns for the broader equity benchmarks, we tend to see broader discount widening in CEFs during more volatile times.

data.title: Metropolitan Bank: Time For A Downgrade After A Phenomenal Run
data.description:
Even the best opportunities eventually hit a point where further market-beating potential becomes limited. That, I would argue, is finally the case when it comes to Metropolitan Bank Holding Corp. (NYSE:MCB). You see, back in May of this year, I reaffirmed the enterprise as a "Buy" candidate. I found myself impressed with the fact that its balance sheet was continuing to grow and that its share price was attractive. The business had successfully exited its digital payments and crypto-related business, leading to a stabilization when it came to its non-interest income and reducing overall volatility for investors. And even though asset quality was not exactly where I wanted it to be, it was attractive enough to justify maintaining it as a bullish prospect, even though I warned that a downgrade was likely if shares continued to rise.

Since that time, the stock has risen a solid 19%. That's more than double the 8.8% increase the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. But that's nothing. When I originally wrote about the company in December 2023, I actually had rated it a "Strong Buy." Since then, the stock has risen by 91.6%. That dwarfs the 41.5% rise the broader market experienced. But now, even with asset quality at pretty solid levels, the company is finally at the point where I believe that downgrading it to a "Hold" is appropriate.

No longer banking on Metropolitan Bank

Perhaps the best place to start with when it comes to Metropolitan Bank would be to cover its overall balance sheet position. During the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, which is the most recent quarter for which we have data available, the bank reported deposits of $6.79 billion. That's up soundly from the $5.98 billion the company ended 2024 with. This represents a strong showing for the company at a time when many financial institutions are struggling to get deposits in the door. High interest rates have caused depositors to look elsewhere for yield. Even non-interest-bearing demand deposits, which would be the most unattractive for depositors, reported an increase from $1.33 billion at the end of last year to $1.43 billion in the second quarter of this year. But the big driver of growth ended up being money market accounts, which grew from $4.51 billion to $5.23 billion as depositors tried to capture those higher payouts.

From the end of last year to the second quarter of this year, the value of loans increased from $5.97 billion to $6.54 billion. As has historically been the case, most of the company’s exposure is to real estate. Primarily, we are talking about commercial real estate, of which the business had exposure to $4.85 billion worth at the end of the most recent quarter. By comparison, other categories are quite small. The next largest would be the commercial and industrial market, with total deposit exposure of $1.02 billion. And in a distant third place would be the $414.2 million that the company has dedicated to multifamily real estate.

Management has made sure to keep some of the cash that the company brings in dedicated to securities as well. Those amounted to $944.2 million for the second quarter of this year. That's up from the $915.8 million that the company ended last year with. Over the same period, cash and cash equivalents went from $200.3 million to $152.5 million. But this is acceptable to me when you consider that, during the same timeframe, cash and cash equivalents were slashed from $478.1 million to $238 million.

We have seen respectable growth as of late on the income statement. For the second quarter of 2025, net interest income for the company came in at $67.3 million. That's up from the $60 million the company had for the same quarter last year. The increase in the company's balance sheet certainly helped here. Another big contributor, however, was a rise in the firm's net interest margin from 3.44% to 3.83%. Things would have been better had it not been for the fact that, year over year, the provision for credit losses incurred by the company grew from $1.5 million to $6.4 million. A similarly large increase for the first half of this year caused overall net interest income to climb more modestly from $119.2 million to $129.7 million.

Non-interest income, meanwhile, has been much more disappointing. In the second quarter of this year, the company generated only $2.6 million from these activities. That's down from the $6.1 million reported the previous year. The culprit here was that, last year, the business generated $3.7 million from its Global Payments Group. This year, it generated nothing from it.

This was an intentional decline, however, as management completed the company's exit from this part of the business, which operated as an issuer of debit cards for third-party debit card programs and which administered both domestic and international digital payment settlements for non-bank financial services clients, back in 2023. Its decision in 2024 to also exit the BaaS side of the business, which, like the rest of this part of the enterprise, focused on the crypto asset space, would naturally cause a decline in revenue. Despite this, the firm still managed to generate net profits in the second quarter of this year of $18.8 million. That's above the $16.8 million reported the previous year and brings results for the first half of 2025 up to $35.1 million, compared to the $33 million the company generated one year earlier.

When it comes to the banking sector, I put a lot of weight on valuation. I typically like to see institutions in this industry trade at a price-to-earnings multiple of 10 or lower. But because of how much shares of the business have risen recently, even if we project out results based on what we have seen so far for the year, we would get a price-to-earnings multiple of 11.5. Admittedly, as the chart above illustrates, this would still place the company below four of the five banks that I decided to compare it to. But still, it is more expensive than I would like it to be. In the chart below, I did the same type of comparative analysis using the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples. On a price-to-book basis, Metropolitan Bank ended up being around the middle of the group, with three of the five companies I stacked it up against being cheaper than it is. But on a price-to-tangible-book basis, only one of the companies was cheaper.

In addition to the stock being more expensive than I would like it to be, asset quality remains mixed. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading of 0.97%. That's just a hair below the 1% minimum that I like to see. But it also places the company around the middle of the group, as the chart illustrates. In the chart below that, you can see the same type of analysis using return on equity. In this case, the 10.40% reading that we get is slightly above the 10% minimum that I like to see. But once again, this places the company around the middle of the group.

Takeaway

Currently, it pains me to have to downgrade Metropolitan Bank. The bank has had a remarkable run over the last couple of years now. It certainly has been one of my better calls in this space. To be honest with you, it's not even a bad call now. But as a value investor, my objective is to find companies that are almost certain to pay off, as measured by achieving performance that is greater than the broader market for the foreseeable future. And at this point, while it is possible that Metropolitan Bank could very well continue to do just that, I don't believe that the margin of safety is sufficient to justify a "Hold" rating at this time.
